attributions.name: Daniel B. M. Haun
attributions.affiliation: Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology, Department of Comparative Cultural Psychology

content.text: Uniquely human cognition refers to aspects of cognition that are specific to our evolutionary lineage and distinguish us from other animals, including our closest living relatives, the nonhuman primates. Understanding these unique aspects embeds theories of human cognition within an evolutionary framework and informs accounts of the foundations of behaviors found in no other species, such as language and religion. Knowledge about uniquely human cognition shapes our understanding of what it means to be human within the broader context of biological species and thereby sets the backdrop for human attitudes toward other animals. This places the cognitive sciences at the forefront of some of the oldest debates in philosophy and broader culture about what makes humans human
Questions of uniquely human cognition have a long history. Across several major traditions, philosophers like Plato (ca. 427
322 B.C.E.), Vasubandhu (ca. fourth century C.E.), and Al-Farabi (ca. 870
950 C.E.) characterized human cognition as uniquely capable of abstract reasoning, moral deliberation, spiritual insight, and future planning. Zhuangzi (ca. 369
286 B.C.E.) challenged this view, suggesting fluid boundaries between species’ cognitive abilities. Much later, the taxonomic label

attrs.unstructuredValue: von Linné, C., & Salvius, L. (1758). Caroli Linnaei...Systema naturae per regna tria naturae : Secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis. Laurentii Salvii. https://doi.org/10.5962/bhl.title.542

content.text: ) reinforced the belief in human cognitive distinctiveness. However, the debate persisted: Descartes described humans as unique “creatures of reason” (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Descartes, R. (1641). Meditationes de prima philosophia, in qua Dei existentia et animæ immortalitas demonstratur. Michael Soly.

content.text: ), whereas Darwin argued that “there is no fundamental difference between man and the higher animals in their mental faculties” (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Darwin, C. (1871). The descent of man, and selection in relation to sex (Vol. 1). John Murray. https://doi.org/10.1037/12293-000

content.text: , pp. viii, 423). This tension carried into the rise of experimental psychology, where comparative studies with other species became a key approach to understanding human cognition (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Wundt, W. M. (1863). Vorlesungen über die Menschen- und Thierseele. Verlag von Leopold Voss.

content.text: traits are features specific to a species, distinguishing it from all others. These traits arise from adaptations that are unique to that species’ evolutionary history. For example, understanding others’ false beliefs is a candidate for a uniquely human cognitive trait (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Call, J., & Tomasello, M. (2008). Does the chimpanzee have a theory of mind? 30 years later. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 12(5), 187–192. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2008.02.010

content.text: traits are unique to a species among its close phylogenetic relatives. Human vocal imitation is an autapomorphic cognitive trait, as it is unique among primates, even though some other mammals (e.g., seals) also exhibit vocal imitation (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Janik, V. M., & Slater, P. J. B. (1997). Vocal learning in mammals. In P. J. B. Slater, J. S. Rosenblatt, C. T. Snowdon, & M. Milinski (Eds.), Advances in the study of behavior (Vol. 26, pp. 59–99). Academic Press. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0065-3454(08)60377-0

content.text: ). Unique and autapomorphic traits are not necessarily universal among individuals [see
] of that species or even common (i.e., species typical). Even if only some individuals of a species have a trait that cannot be found in any other species, it is a unique trait (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Haun, D. B. M. (2015). Comparative and developmental anthropology: Studying the origins of cultural variability in cognitive function. In L. A. Jensen (Ed.), The Oxford handbook of human development and culture: An interdisciplinary perspective (pp. 94–110). Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordhb/9780199948550.013.7

content.text: Differences between human cognition and that of other animals can be

(e.g., only humans understand false beliefs). Distinguishing between these kinds of differences is challenging. Critically, gradual quantitative differences in some cognitive traits can lead to qualitative differences in others. For example, gradually increased memory capacity can result in categorically better pattern detection: To recognize “ABAB” requires remembering at least four items. Animals with a memory capacity of fewer than four items would be incapable to recognize it (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Cantlon, J. F., & Piantadosi, S. T. (2024). Uniquely human intelligence arose from expanded information capacity. Nature Reviews Psychology, 3(4), 275–293. https://doi.org/10.1038/s44159-024-00283-3
Simon, H. A. (1969). The sciences of the artificial. MIT Press.

content.text: ), meaning it arises from interactions among cognitive traits, not simply their sum. Therefore, comparing
across species is essential. Even if all individual human cognitive traits exist elsewhere, their unique co-occurrence in humans may distinguish human cognition (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Laland, K., & Seed, A. (2021). Understanding human cognitive uniqueness. Annual Review of Psychology, 72(1), 689–716. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-psych-062220-051256

content.text: accounts propose that human cognition consists of evolutionarily adaptive, functionally specific, innate input analyzers also called cognitive modules (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Fodor, J. A. (1983). The modularity of mind. MIT Press. https://doi.org/10.7551/mitpress/4737.001.0001

content.text: ). Some of these innate modules are claimed to be uniquely human, such as recursive representation (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Dehaene, S., Al Roumi, F., Lakretz, Y., Planton, S., & Sablé-Meyer, M. (2022). Symbols and mental programs: A hypothesis about human singularity. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 26(9), 751–766. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2022.06.010

content.text: the ability to identify violations of social exchange rules (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Cosmides, L., Tooby, J., Fiddick, L., & Bryant, G. A. (2005). Detecting cheaters. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 9(11), 505–506. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2005.09.005

content.text: accounts suggest that human cognition arises not from single-purpose adaptations but broad cognitive abilities affecting multiple domains, emphasizing learning. For example, unique understanding of relations between relations (e.g., A is to A as B is to B) influences abstract thinking, causal reasoning, metacognition, theory of mind, and cultural innovation (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Penn, D. C., Holyoak, K. J., & Povinelli, D. J. (2008). Darwin’s mistake: Explaining the discontinuity between human and nonhuman minds. Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 31(2), 109–130. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0140525X08003543

content.text: accounts propose that human cognition is rooted in innate developmental primitives that organize along core knowledge domains like objects, numbers, and agents (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Spelke, E. (2022). What babies know: Core knowledge and composition. (Vol. 1). Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780190618247.001.0001

content.text: ). Core knowledge is evolutionarily ancient and shared with other animals. Uniquely human cognition arises through recombination and extension of their function throughout ontogeny (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Carey, S. (2009). The origin of concepts. Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/acprof:oso/9780195367638.001.0001

content.text: theories reject strong innateness. They argue that uniquely human cognition emerges from gradually constructing knowledge via interaction with the environment (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Piaget, J. (1952). The origins of intelligence in children. (M. Cook, Trans.). W. W. Norton & Company. https://doi.org/10.1037/11494-000
Vygotsky, L. S. (1978). Mind in society: The development of higher psychological processes. Harvard University Press. https://doi.org/10.2307/j.ctvjf9vz4

content.text: ) rather than from recombining cognitive modules. Core knowledge accounts have stronger domain specificity constraints than constructivist accounts.

In cultural intelligence accounts of human cognition, it evolved primarily to handle the challenges of living in complex, cooperative, socially organized groups. Theories in this category emphasize the central role of social politics (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Byrne, R., & Whiten, A. (1989). Machiavellian intelligence: Social expertise and the evolution of intellect in monkeys, apes, and humans. Oxford University Press.
Tomasello, M. (2009). Why we cooperate. MIT Press. https://doi.org/10.7551/mitpress/8470.001.0001

Henrich, J. (2015). The secret of our success: How culture is driving human evolution, domesticating our species, and making us smarter. Princeton University Press. https://doi.org/10.2307/j.ctvc77f0d
Heyes, C. (2018). Cognitive gadgets: The cultural evolution of thinking. Harvard University Press. https://doi.org/10.4159/9780674985155

Burkart, J. M., Hrdy, S. B., & Van Schaik, C. P. (2009). Cooperative breeding and human cognitive evolution. Evolutionary Anthropology: Issues, News, and Reviews, 18(5), 175–186. https://doi.org/10.1002/evan.20222

content.text: ) as drivers of uniquely human cognition. Cognitive differences between humans and other animals in these accounts are often quantitative. Importantly, these quantitative differences enable culture, which drives qualitative species-level cognitive differences.
Many domain-general accounts emphasize the role of language in shaping uniquely human cognition, whether through an innate “mentalese” system that structures all thought (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Fodor, J. A. (1975). The language of thought. Harvard University Press.

content.text: ) or through language as a necessary tool for higher-order cognition, including, for example, abstract reasoning (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Vygotsky, L. S. (1978). Mind in society: The development of higher psychological processes. Harvard University Press. https://doi.org/10.2307/j.ctvjf9vz4
Dennett, D. C. (1991). Consciousness explained. Little, Brown and Co.

Gentner, D. (2003). Why we’re so smart. In D. Gentner & S. Goldin-Meadow (Eds.), Language in mind: Advances in the study of language and thought (pp. 195–235). MIT Press. https://doi.org/10.7551/mitpress/4117.003.0015

content.text: ]. In core knowledge accounts of cognitive development, language enables the recombination and extension of core knowledge modules (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Carey, S. (2009). The origin of concepts. Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/acprof:oso/9780195367638.001.0001

content.text: For slowly developing species with complex cognition, such as primates, understanding within-species variation is crucial before making species-level comparisons (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Bohn, M., Fong, F. T. K., Pope-Caldwell, S., Stengelin, R., & Haun, D. B. M. (2024). Understanding cultural variation in cognition one child at a time. Nature Reviews Psychology, 3, 641–643. https://doi.org/10.1038/s44159-024-00351-8

Haun, D. B. M. (2015). Comparative and developmental anthropology: Studying the origins of cultural variability in cognitive function. In L. A. Jensen (Ed.), The Oxford handbook of human development and culture: An interdisciplinary perspective (pp. 94–110). Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/oxfordhb/9780199948550.013.7

Nielsen, M., & Haun, D. (2016). Why developmental psychology is incomplete without comparative and cross-cultural perspectives. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B, 371(1686), 20150071. https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.2015.0071

content.text: ). Yet, within-species variation is commonly underestimated when comparing cognition between species. Human cognition is often inferred from studies with psychology students in the Global North (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Henrich, J., Heine, S. J., & Norenzayan, A. (2010). The weirdest people in the world? Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 33(2–3), 61–83. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0140525X0999152X

content.text: ]. For instance, it was long assumed that all humans share a universal capacity
for exact, symbolic number, distinguishing humans from other animals (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Gelman, R., & Gallistel, C. R. (1978). The child’s understanding of number. Harvard University Press.

content.text: ). However, cross-cultural studies have shown that such number concepts depend on cultural tools like numerical language, making them uniquely human, but not universal (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Frank, M. C., Everett, D. L., Fedorenko, E., & Gibson, E. (2008). Number as a cognitive technology: Evidence from Pirahã language and cognition. Cognition, 108(3), 819–824. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2008.04.007

content.text: ). Similarly, models of animal cognition often rely on small, homogeneous samples in specific living conditions (

attrs.unstructuredValue: van Leeuwen, E. J. C., Cronin, K. A., & Haun, D. B. M. (2018). Population-specific social dynamics in chimpanzees. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 115(45), 11393–11400. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1722614115

content.text: ). Chimpanzees, for example, have been characterized as less socially tolerant in contrast to other primates, especially humans (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Hrdy, S. B. (2011). Mothers and others. Harvard University Press. https://doi.org/10.2307/j.ctt1c84czb

content.text: ). However, recent findings have shown that chimpanzee social tolerance varies with demographic conditions, producing within-species variation that exceeds proposed between-species differences (

attrs.unstructuredValue: DeTroy, S. E., Ross, C. T., Cronin, K. A., van Leeuwen, E. J. C., & Haun, D. B. M. (2021). Cofeeding tolerance in chimpanzees depends on group composition: A longitudinal study across four communities. iScience, 24(3), 102175. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isci.2021.102175

content.text: Human cognition is unique not just when compared to other intelligent biological species but also when compared to artificial intelligences. While synthetic systems now make decisions, create art, and solve scientific problems (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Brinkmann, L., Baumann, F., Bonnefon, J.-F., Derex, M., Müller, T. F., Nussberger, A.-M., Czaplicka, A., Acerbi, A., Griffiths, T. L., Henrich, J., Leibo, J. Z., McElreath, R., Oudeyer, P.-Y., Stray, J., & Rahwan, I. (2023). Machine culture. Nature Human Behaviour, 7(11), 1855–1868. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-023-01742-2

content.text: ), they do so in ways that are different from the human approach to the same challenges (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Griffiths, T. L., Chater, N., & Tenenbaum, J. B. (2024). Bayesian models of cognition: Reverse engineering the mind. MIT Press.

content.text: ]. Understanding these differences and commonalities constitutes a new comparative psychology, including biological and synthetic species.
Questions about human cognitive uniqueness are central to many fields within the cognitive sciences [see

]. Recently, they have gained significance in artificial intelligence, aiming to synthesize uniquely human intelligence, and in animal ethics, where the value of animal life is evaluated based on their capacity for human-like thoughts.
From bacteria to Bach and back: The evolution of minds
Laland, K., & Seed, A. (2021). Understanding human cognitive uniqueness.
The primate origins of human nature
